perturbation.original_text: 5.3.1 Within thirty (30) days of the end of each calendar quarter during the Term, the Company will, and Online BVI will cause the Company to, furnish to Skype complete and accurate statements (each, a "Statement") in a form acceptable to Skype, certified to be accurate by an officer of the Company or Online BVI, as applicable, showing with reasonable detail (i) the Gross Revenue actually received and collected by the Company or Online BVI, or if applicable, any Subsidiary or other Affiliate (including, without limitation, a Subsidiary or other Affiliate of Tom Holding), licensee, distributor or reseller thereof, during the preceding calendar quarter and on a cumulative basis, (ii) the Direct Expenses incurred thereby, during the preceding calendar quarter and on a cumulative basis, and (iii) the calculation of Adjusted Net Revenue.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.3.1 Within ninety (90) days of the end of each calendar quarter during the Term, the Company will, and Online BVI will cause the Company to, furnish to Skype complete and accurate statements (each, a "Statement") in a form acceptable to Skype, certified to be accurate by an officer of the Company or Online BVI, as applicable, showing with reasonable detail (i) the Gross Revenue actually received and collected by the Company or Online BVI, or if applicable, any Subsidiary or other Affiliate (including, without limitation, a Subsidiary or other Affiliate of Tom Holding), licensee, distributor or reseller thereof, during the preceding calendar quarter and on a cumulative basis, (ii) the Direct Expenses incurred thereby, during the preceding calendar quarter and on a cumulative basis, and (iii) the calculation of Adjusted Net Revenue.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the reporting deadline from 30 days to 90 days may cause issues with timely financial reporting and compliance, particularly if the companies involved are publicly traded in the US, such as Tom Holding, which is listed on NASDAQ. This could introduce regulatory non-compliance, which is a perturbation.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 CFR § 240.13a-13 specifies deadlines for filing quarterly reports (Form 10-Q). Specifically, Rule 13a-13(a) requires these reports to be filed within 40 or 45 days after the end of each of the first three fiscal quarters for larger accelerated filers and smaller reporting companies. The modified text of 90 days contradicts these SEC filing deadlines, creating a violation for any publicly traded company subject to these regulations, like Tom Holding.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.13a-13 - Quarterly reports on Form 10-Q (§ 249.308a of this chapter). | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13a-13 - Quarterly reports on Form 10-Q (§ 249.308a of this chapter). CFR prev | next § 240.13a-13 Quarterly reports on Form 10-Q (§ 249.308a of this chapter). (a) Except as provided in paragraphs (b) and (c) of this section , every issuer that has securities registered pursuant to section 12 of the Act and is required to file annual reports pursuant to section 13 of the Act, and has filed or intends to file such reports on Form 10-K ( § 249.310 of this chapter), shall file a quarterly report on Form 10-Q (§ 249.308a of this chapter) within the period specified in General Instruction A.1. to that form for each of the first three quarters of each fiscal year of the issuer , commencing with the first fiscal quarter following the most recent fiscal year for which full financial statements were included in the registration statement , or, if the registration statement included financial statements for an interim period subsequent to the most recent fiscal year end meeting the requirements of Article 10 of Regulation S-X and Rule 8-03 of Regulation S-X for smaller reporting companies, for the first fiscal quarter subsequent to the quarter reported upon in the registration statement . The first quarterly report of the issuer shall be filed either within 45 days after the effective date of the registration statement or on or before the date on which such report would have been required to be filed if the issuer has been required to file reports on Form 10-Q as of its last fiscal quarter, whichever is later. (b) The provisions of this rule shall not apply to the following issuers: (1) Investment companies required to file reports pursuant to § 270.30a-1 ; (2) Foreign private issuers required to file reports pursuant to § 240.13a-16 ; and (3) Asset-backed issu
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the deadline for furnishing financial statements from 30 days to 90 days. The Law Explanation states that 17 CFR § 240.13a-13 requires these reports to be filed within 40 or 45 days after the end of each of the first three fiscal quarters for larger accelerated filers and smaller reporting companies. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms this, stating that issuers required to file annual reports on Form 10-K shall file a quarterly report on Form 10-Q within the period specified in General Instruction A.1. to that form. By extending the deadline to 90 days, the modified text creates a clear inconsistency with SEC filing deadlines, potentially causing the affected companies to violate federal law. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 5.3.2 Interest shall be payable on any amounts paid later than the date due hereunder at the prime rate as reported by the New York edition of the Wall Street Journal on the day the amount is due calculated from the date any amount is due until the date of receipt of the relevant sum by the applicable Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.3.2 Interest shall be payable on any amounts paid later than the date due hereunder at a rate of 25% per annum, calculated from the date any amount is due until the date of receipt of the relevant sum by the applicable Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the interest rate to 25% per annum can be considered usurious depending on the jurisdiction and the nature of the transaction. Usury laws are in place to prevent excessively high interest rates on loans and debts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 891 - Definitions and rules of construction
perturbation.law_explanation: While 18 U.S. Code § 891 defines extortionate credit transactions, various state laws set specific usury limits. A 25% interest rate could violate these state usury laws depending on the context of the agreement and the applicable state's regulations. Laws about usury varies across each State, some having no explicit interest rate ceiling, some being higher. For example, many states cap interest rates between 6 and 10 percent, while others allow for significantly higher rates, especially for transactions between corporations, or permit fees that exceed the usury rate limit. The specified 25% could violate various state limits.

Definitions and rules of construction For the purposes of this chapter: (1) To extend credit means to make or renew any loan, or to enter into any agreement, tacit or express, whereby the repayment or satisfaction of any debt or claim, whether acknowledged or disputed, valid or invalid, and however arising, may or will be deferred. (2) The term "creditor", with reference to any given extension of credit, refers to any person making
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces an interest rate based on the Wall Street Journal's prime rate with a fixed rate of 25% per annum. The Law Explanation indicates that a 25% interest rate could violate state usury laws, depending on the context and state regulations. The Perturbation Explanation also states that this could be usurious depending on the jurisdiction. Snippet 1, 18 USC 891, defines credit transactions but does not provide specific rate limitations; the Law Explanation clarifies that usury limits are defined by state laws. Because the modified text could violate state laws it creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.4 Audit Rights. The Parties (including Online BVI on behalf of the Online Group) agree to maintain records (i) of all information reasonably necessary to verify all calculations to be made under Section 8.3.2, and (ii) supporting, verifying and necessary to demonstrate the calculation and collection of fees and/or revenue, as well as any deductions thereto, and payments made hereunder, including, without limitation, budgets, purchase orders, expense records, invoices, correspondence, banking and financial and other records pertaining to the determination of Gross Revenue, Direct Expenses and Adjusted Net Revenue, during the term of this Agreement and for a period of two (2) years following the expiration or termination hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.4 Audit Rights. The Parties (including Online BVI on behalf of the Online Group) agree to maintain records (i) of all information reasonably necessary to verify all calculations to be made under Section 8.3.2, and (ii) supporting, verifying and necessary to demonstrate the calculation and collection of fees and/or revenue, as well as any deductions thereto, and payments made hereunder, including, without limitation, budgets, purchase orders, expense records, invoices, correspondence, banking and financial and other records pertaining to the determination of Gross Revenue, Direct Expenses and Adjusted Net Revenue, during the term of this Agreement and for a period of six (6) months following the expiration or termination hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record retention period to six months may conflict with statutory requirements for financial recordkeeping, especially considering that Tom Online Inc. is listed on the NASDAQ National Market in the United States. This could lead to non-compliance with relevant regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 - Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers
perturbation.law_explanation: SEC Rule 17a-4 outlines record preservation requirements for brokers and dealers. While this specific rule might not directly apply to Tom Online Inc. in its entirety, it indicates the general expectation for retaining financial records within the US regulatory framework. The reduced six-month period is substantially shorter than typical requirements (often three to six years) for tax and audit purposes, creating a conflict because it could impede regulatory oversight, especially if the co-branding agreement generates revenues that are subject to scrutiny and regulatory requirements. This is relevant given that Tom Online Inc. is a NASDAQ-listed company.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 - Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.17a-4 - Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.17a-4 Records to be preserved by certain exchange members, brokers and dealers. This section applies to the following types of entities: A member of a national securities exchange who transacts a business in securities directly with others than members of a national securities exchange ; a broker or dealer who transacts a business in securities through the medium of a member of a national securities exchange ; a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer as that term is defined in § 240.3b-12 , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78 o ); a security-based swap dealer registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78 o -10) that is also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act; and a major security-based swap participant registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act. Section 240.18a-6 (rather than this section) applies to the following types of entities: A security-based swap dealer registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is not also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act; and a major security-based swap participant registered pursuant to section 15F of the Act that is not also a broker or dealer, including an OTC derivatives dealer , registered pursuant to section 15 of the Act. (a) Every member , broker or dealer subject to § 240.17a-3 must preserve for a period of not less than 6 years, the first two years
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the record retention period from two years to six months following the expiration or termination of the agreement. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this could conflict with regulatory expectations for financial recordkeeping, particularly for a NASDAQ-listed company like Tom Online Inc. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 17 CFR § 240.17a-4, states that members, brokers, or dealers subject to § 240.17a-3 must preserve records for at least six years, with the first two years being readily accessible. Although this snippet refers specifically to brokers and dealers, the Law Explanation suggests it represents a general expectation for financial record retention. The reduction to six months creates a conflict with this expectation, thus creating an inconsistency between the contract and potential regulatory requirements.
